title: How to Convert AI Generated Images into Vector SVG
description:
In today's video, I'm showing you how to turn pictures made by a computer program into vector files that you can cut or print. We'll use Illustrate Ai to make some fun pictures, then clean them up in Photoshop. Finally, we'll use Illustrator to turn them into vector files. Follow along to learn how to do it yourself!

Join Our Community
https://www.facebook.com/groups/pixaromacommunity

Chapters:
00:00 Part 1: Image Generation - Illustrate Ai
03:55 Part 2: Image Preparation - Photoshop
10:39 Part 3: Image Vectorization - Illustrator
transcript: Welcome to another video tutorial. Today I want to talk about something people have been  asking me for a while now, how to convert my AI generated image into a vector SVG cut file.  In this video, I'll be using Illustrate AI from Design Bundles, Adobe Photoshop and Adobe Illustrator.  Let's get started.  Using Illustrate AI, let's add a positive prompt for a pumpkin.  The goal here is to get simple shapes so they can be vectorized more easily.  I'm using LineArt as the art style and generating four images.  I've created a list of words for the negative prompt to improve the chances of getting simple,  colorless images. As you can see, I've included words like gradients, shading, and colors to specify  what I don't want. It doesn't guarantee these things won't appear, but it does reduce the chances.  It takes a while to generate the images, so I've skipped some parts in the video.  To publish the images you like, use the Publish and Upscale button.  After it's ready, you'll see the word published in green.  Then you can find the green download button, which lets you download the final image to your computer.  I'm going to generate a few more options and try some more prompts.  Once I have a few examples, we'll move on to the next part.  Let's move on to part two, where we'll prepare the images in Adobe Photoshop.  I'll open the first example with the pumpkin in Photoshop.  As you can see, it has a gradient background.  To get it ready for vectorizing, we want a white background.  Use the Object Selection tool to select just the pumpkin.  Next, create a new layer and go to the Select menu to choose Inverse.  This will flip our selection so only the background is selected.  Then go to Edit and Choose Fill, filling it with the color white.  If you already have white selected, you can also just hit Alt plus Backspace.  Go back to the Select menu and choose Deselect to get rid of the selection.  To make the image look even cleaner, you can use the Levels option to tweak the white and black.  Just play around with the sliders on the left and right until you're happy with how it looks.  When you're ready, you can flatten the image by right-clicking.  You can zoom in and out using the Shortcuts Control plus plus and Control plus minus.  Use a hard round brush to paint over the parts you don't want in white  and use black for the parts you do want to keep.  To switch between black and white quickly while painting, you can use the Shortcut X.  Some of these lines are too thin and not needed, so I'll use white to paint over them and remove them.  Let's move on to the second example with the Gorilla. As you can see, this one has more gradients and even some color.  First, add a black and white adjustment layer to get rid of the color.  Then use Levels to reduce some of the gradient.  Just play with the sliders until the image looks clearer.  After that, right-click and flatten the image.  Use the Object Selection Tool or any other selection tool you prefer.  If you're not sure how to do this, I have a video called Remove Background from AI Images that can help.  Just like with the pumpkin, you'll want to invert the selection and fill it with white, then deselect it.  Use the same hard brush to paint over small details in black or white.  We want to make the design as simple as possible, so it's easier to turn it into a vector later on.  The more time you spend on this, the better your chances are of getting a clean vector file.  Let's move on to Part 3, where we'll turn the images into vector format using Adobe Illustrator.  Create a new document in the size you want your project to be.  For this example, I'll make a 6-inch document.  Then go to File and Choose Place, or you can just drag the image into Illustrator.  Use Ctrl plus plus and Ctrl plus minus to zoom in and out.  To resize the image, hold down the Shift key to keep it in proportion, and drag from the corner to make it fit your workspace.  Go to the Image Trace menu, click on the arrow and pick a profile.  Let's see what happens when I choose Line Art.  If your computer is slow, you might want to make the image smaller before you use the Trace feature.  As you can see, the result isn't great, it caught some lines, but not exactly how I wanted.  To fix this, go to the Image Trace panel.  This is where you can really fine-tune things.  You can try out different presets to see what looks best.  There's an advanced section with even more sliders for you to play around with.  I usually go with the black and white mode and then move the sliders to get the look I want.  There's also a handy option called Ignore White, which will leave you with just the black shape.  Save these settings as your starting point.  What works for me most times is to have low noise and more paths, depending on the design.  And don't forget to check Ignore White.  Once you're happy with how it looks, you can save these settings for next time by clicking  Manage Presets and then Save as New Preset.  Let's name it SVGReady.  Now, that preset will show up in the list for next time.  Finally, click Expand to turn your image into a vector.  If you zoom in and move around, you'll see that it's not a regular image anymore,  it's made of points and lines that you can change.  After you click Expand, you'll often find that your shape has a lot of points.  This can make it harder to edit the shape and could take longer for a cutting machine to work with.  To fix this, go to Object, then Path, and then click Simplify.  If you click on the three dots, you'll find Sliders to adjust how much you simplify the shape.  You can change the curve and corner points.  The screen will also show you how many points are in the original shape and how many will be left after simplifying.  It's up to you how much you want to reduce.  Some shapes can handle more simplification than others without looking messed up.  The idea is to remove just enough points so that the shape still looks good but is easier to work with.  Usually, the Auto Simplify option does a pretty good job.  I forgot to mention, make sure to check your layers so you don't have any extra tiny shapes or dots that you don't want.  If you expand the group in the Layers panel, you can hide each object one at a time to see what part of the design it is.  In my next video, I'll show you an example where you might end up with tiny shapes that are hard to spot.  It's also a good idea to clean up your work.  To do this, go to Object, then Path, and then Clean Up.  This will get rid of straight points, empty text paths, and unpainted objects if you have any.  It's a good way to make sure everything is neat and tidy.  I opened the SVG file in Silhouette Studio and everything looks good.  If you go to the Send menu, you can see in red where the cuts will be made.  As you can see, it's a transparent vector design, which means you can make it bigger or smaller as you need, without losing any quality.  Let's move on to the next project featuring the Gorilla. Adding voice over to everything I do takes a lot of time,  so if there's something you don't get, feel free to join the Pixaroma Community Facebook group.  I'll answer your questions there.  Thanks for watching and I'll see you in the next one.
